title: The new news reality: Bigger reach, lower trust
tldr: News reaches more people than ever, yet trust keeps falling — a paradox reshaping how communicators earn credibility. Directly relevant to how people consume media today.
angle: Write about why the trust deficit, not reach, is the real content challenge of 2026.

title: 7-hours-a-day screen habit presents dilemma for brands
tldr: IPA TouchPoints data shows screen time rising while trust declines — a direct look at shifting media-consumption habits. Useful ammunition for content strategists.
angle: Explore what a 7-hour screen day plus falling trust means for where content should actually live.

title: ‘A Super Bowl every two days’: Inside Unilever’s 50,000-creator World Cup play
tldr: Unilever mobilized 50,000 creators for a sustained World Cup content blitz — a concrete campaign breakdown at massive scale. A case study in distributed, human-led content.
angle: Break down what 50,000 creators teach us about content ops and governance at scale.

title: How to Write Content That Lands With Decision Makers
tldr: Output is up but senior buyers are less impressed than ever; a practical guide to thought leadership that actually moves contract-signers. Cuts against volume-obsessed content programs.
angle: Argue that the impressions-vs-impact gap is content strategy's biggest blind spot.

title: How Time and others are rebuilding parts of the web for AI agents
tldr: Publishers are building AI-friendly versions of their sites to stay discoverable in agentic search — a structural shift in how content gets found and consumed.
angle: Write about content architecture for a web where AI agents, not humans, are the first readers.

title: Mercury Will Pay $335K to Reframe a Category. The Job Description Reads Like an Editor-in-Chief's.
tldr: A fintech's $335K hire looks more like an editor-in-chief than a marketer — signaling brands treating category narrative as an editorial function. A telling shift in corporate publishing.
angle: Explore why brands are hiring editors-in-chief to own category narrative.

title: Everything in Marketing Still Works. It Just Doesn't Differentiate You Anymore.
tldr: A provocation that every proven tactic now works for everyone, so tactics no longer differentiate — pushing brands back toward distinctiveness and narrative. A useful strategic reframe.
angle: Make the case that in a world where all tactics work, only narrative differentiates.

title: Is Your Brand Loud And Wrong? What World Cup Content Exposes
tldr: World Cup content reveals which brands understand fandom versus those just shouting — a timely critique of tone-deaf, high-volume brand content. Good case-study fodder.
angle: Analyze what separates brands that join a cultural moment from those that hijack it.

title: Getty Images says finance brands need more realistic visuals
tldr: Getty argues finance brands need more human, realistic imagery over polished stock — a data point in the shift toward people-over-polish visual content.
angle: Write on why realistic, human visuals now outperform aspirational polish.

title: AI-Generated Marketing Is Eroding Customer Trust. Are Brands Measuring the Wrong Things?
tldr: AI-generated content may boost output while quietly eroding trust — and brands may be measuring the wrong metrics. Ties human-centered content to measurement.
angle: Argue that trust erosion from AI content is invisible in standard dashboards.

title: Inside Alex Cooper's Cannes pitch—direct creator-brand partnerships, no agency middleman
tldr: Alex Cooper is pitching brands to work with creators directly, cutting out agencies — a structural signal for the creator economy and media buying.
angle: Examine what direct creator-brand deals mean for owned vs earned media strategy.

title: How I Never Run Out of LinkedIn Content Ideas
tldr: A practitioner's system for a sustainable LinkedIn idea pipeline — useful for personal/executive publishing on the platform where B2B thought leadership lives.
angle: Share your own repeatable system for turning work into a content pipeline.

title: Why would a hotel brand sponsor a Coachella docuseries?
tldr: A breakdown of a hotel brand backing a Coachella docuseries — a concrete example of brands funding entertainment content over traditional ads.
angle: Explore when brands should fund content universes instead of running campaigns.

title: Grief has entered the workplace. Here's what leaders can do
tldr: Leaders now guide teams through layoffs, AI disruption and loss; a case for naming grief in internal communications. Human-centered angle on employee engagement.
angle: Write about grief and loss as an internal communications responsibility, not an HR afterthought.

title: 'Should we do it at all?' The question David Johnston wants every creative to ask
tldr: Accept & Proceed's founder introduces 'Signalism,' a framework asking whether creative work should exist at all — a purpose-first challenge to content production.
angle: Apply the 'should we make this at all?' filter to content strategy and volume.
